description: This test case evaluates the generator model's accuracy and technical depth, assuming a perfect retrieval process.
question: Explain how the image exposure apparatus described in Section 1 (Source: US7123417) utilizes the rotation of the digital micromirror device (DMD) to enhance resolution during exposure, and discuss the role of the fiber-array light source and condensing lens system in achieving uniform light distribution on the DMD.

gold_context_sources.evidence: Further, it is preferable that the DMD 50 is slightly rotated within the plane parallel to the DMD 50 so that the shorter side of the DMD 50 is at a predetermined angle 6 (e.g., 1 to 5 degrees) with the subscanning direction. FIG. 8A shows scanning traces of exposure beams (light images) 53 reflected by the respective micromirrors in the case where the DMD 50 is not rotated, and FIG. 8B shows scanning traces of exposure beams 53 reflected by the respective micromirrors in the case where the DMD 50 is rotated. which are two-dimensionally arranged. Since a great num- ber of portions of light which have passed through the first micro fly-eye lens array 72 and the second micro fly-eye lens array 73 are superimposed on each other and applied to the DMD 50, the distribution of the quantity of light applied to the DMD 50 becomes uniform.

In the DMD 50, a great number of micromirror arrays (e.g., 756 micromirror arrays) are arranged in the direction of the shorter side, and a great number of micromirrors (e.g., 1,024 micromirrors) are arranged in each micromirror array which extends in the direction of the longer side of the DMD 50. As illustrated in FIG. 8B, the pitch P, of the scanning lines (scanning traces) of the exposure beams 53 reflected by the respective micromirrors in the case where the DMD 50 is appropriately rotated within the plane parallel to the DMD 50 becomes smaller than the pitch P, of the scanning lines of the exposure beams 53 reflected by the respective micromirrors in the case where the DMD 50 is not rotated within the plane parallel to the DMD 50. Therefore, the resolution can be greatly increased by the rotation of the DMD 50. In addition, since the rotation angle of the DMD 50 is very small, the scanning width W, in the case where the DMD 50 is rotated within the plane parallel to the DMD 50 is not substantially changed from the scanning width W, in the case where the DMD 50 is not rotated within the plane parallel to the DMD 50.

On the light-entrance side of the DMD 50, a fiber-array light source 66, a condensing lens system 67, and a mirror 69 are arranged in this order. The fiber-array light source 66 has a laser emission portion in which light-emission end portions (light-emission points) of optical fibers are arranged along a line in the direction of the longer side of the exposure area. The condensing lens system 67 shapes laser light emitted from the fiber-array light source 66, and condenses the shaped laser light on the DMD. The mirror 69 reflects toward the DMD 50 the laser light which has passed through the condensing lens system 67. In FIG. 4, the condensing lens system 67 is schematically indicated. ... Since a great num- ber of portions of light which have passed through the first micro fly-eye lens array 72 and the second micro fly-eye lens array 73 are superimposed on each other and applied to the DMD 50, the distribution of the quantity of light applied to the DMD 50 becomes uniform.

ground_truth_answer:
The image exposure apparatus uses a digital micromirror device (DMD) composed of a large matrix of micromirrors that can be individually controlled to reflect light according to pixel data. To enhance resolution during exposure, the DMD is slightly rotated within its plane by a small predetermined angle (typically 1 to 5 degrees) relative to the subscanning direction. This rotation causes the scanning lines (or traces) of the exposure beams reflected by the micromirrors to have a smaller pitch compared to the non-rotated case. By effectively decreasing the pitch of these scanning lines, the apparatus achieves a higher spatial resolution without substantially changing the overall scanning width. This fine rotation allows for more precise control of exposure positions, enabling higher definition images on the photosensitive material.

The fiber-array light source and the condensing lens system play critical roles in uniformly illuminating the DMD. The fiber-array light source consists of multiple optical fibers arranged so that their light-emission points form a line along the longer side of the exposure area. The condensing lens system, which includes elements such as collimator lenses and micro fly-eye lens arrays, shapes and superimposes the laser light emitted from the fiber-array light source. This optical arrangement ensures that numerous portions of light are combined and uniformly distributed onto the DMD surface. The uniform light distribution is essential for consistent exposure across the DMD, thus contributing to the quality and uniformity of the final image formed on the photosensitive sheet.
